channel.title: All Climbs on Scottish Winter Climbing
channel.description: Recent content in All Climbs on Scottish Winter Climbing

item.title: The Godfather
item.description:
11th March 2023, Coire Nan Fhamhair, Beinn Bhan

The Godfather has some of the richest history of all modern winter climbs in Scotland. All these legends were swirling around up there when Rob and I dared to finally give it a try last winter, and so to set the scene I’d like to re-tell some of the stories.

Martin Moran lived around the corner and had eyed up the line for years around the turn of the millennium. While the top half of the route is a huge and obvious corner, the bottom half of the giant’s wall is just a series of overhanging blank walls. The crag is wet and vegetated despite the angle, so this is winter-only terrain. In 2002 he teamed up with Paul Tattershall to finally give it a shot. After a series of testing pitches they arrived underneath the final corner in the dark. From Martin’s fantastic account in Guy Robertson’s book, Great Mountain Crags of Scotland:

item.title: Hung, Drawn, and Quartered
item.description:
8th March 2023, Am Basteir

We’ve reached the penultimate route of last winter now, which occurred on another one of those “best day of the winter” days. This being on Wednesday, I secured yet another last minute day off from my long-suffering manager and plans were set in motion!

Iain and I headed over from the fort, meeting up with Rob at Kyle of Lochalsh where we spent the night in the vans in a secret otter-friendly car park. We could see the hills on Skye gleaming white in the moonlight in the distance. So far so good..

item.title: Rumplestiltskin
item.description:
5th March 2023, Carn Etchachan

I wanted to put this short post up to rave about the quality of a little-known route called Rumplestilskin on the Carn Etchachan upper tier, which Rob and I headed up to one Sunday. It was Rob’s 10th successive day out winter climbing, on a run which we’d started on the Gathering the weekend before, and for the first time ever I did detect a hint of weariness! Somehow he was still enthusiastic about walking all the way into the Loch Avon basin..

item.title: Nathrach Dubh
item.description:
26th February 2023, Carn Etchachan

Some photos from one of the best days of the winter, when Tim and I headed over to Carn Etchachan and climbed Nathrach Dubh (aka. Black Snake). Walking over the tops was pretty special as we watched cloud pour into the Loch Avon basin.

I would say that I’ve become a bit of a Carn Etchachan devotee over the past couple of winters, even though I’ve only scratched the surface of the routes there. In the gorgeous weather, Tim and I spent ages scoping out almost every route in the first two amphitheatres as we traversed in. Since unrepeated 1* grade VIs are in vogue these days, someone should go and have a look at Pythagoras (https://www.ukclimbing.com/logbook/crags/ben_macdui_-_carn_etchachan-14648/pythagoras-61007). From where we were standing, it looked wildly unlikely at that grade!

item.title: The Gathering
item.description:
24th February 2023, Coire an Lochain

Warning: what follows is just a move-by-move account of an awesome route, but it was an intense experience for me, and probably the hardest winter pitch I’ve done or will ever do! I owe Will a lot for encouraging Rob and I to get on something like this, as it isn’t really a route I ever considered until recently. It was the start of a pretty special run of big routes with Rob over the following couple of weeks.

item.title: Raven's Edge
item.description:
20th January 2023, Buachaille Etive Mor

7AM: Alarms blare in unison throughout the Caol climbing commune. Three fried eggs and we’re off.

8:45AM: The i20 rolls into the layby and we walk out, it’s a perfect winter day. The path is frozen into an icy runway promising perfect conditions on the hill, it looks like the real deal.

9:30AM: Reality strikes and we realise that most of the Buachaille is rapidly stripping in the blazing sunshine! Not something you normally have to account for. We have a quick brainstorm and casually decide on Raven’s Edge. Rob is the only one of us who knows a bit more about the reputation of Raven’s Edge, and I sensed a bit of trepidation from him at the time, which makes me laugh now. He was definitely right!

item.title: Supersleuth
item.description:
16th December 2022, Mainreachan Buttress on Fuar Tholl

Fuar Tholl hosts a couple of the mythical big crags in the North West which I’d read about in the guidebook for years, but the opportunity to go there had never quite materialised. Towards the end of the long and very cold spell of weather in December, Rob and I made a speculative plan to meet up there, and after finishing work on Thursday I made the long drive from Edinburgh by myself. The thermometer on the car dropped to double digits over Drumochter pass, but my optimism took a hit when Rob reported heavy snow to the road in Glen Carron. I got as far as Garve with everything looking very dry, but with every junction from that point the road was buried deeper and deeper. The only vehicles getting up and down the Glen Carron road were the indefagitable Tesco delivery vans. It has become an ongoing joke seeing these vans charging up and down roads covered in feet of snow in the North West..

item.title: Slav Route
item.description:
4th March 2022, Orion Face, Ben Nevis

For me personally, much of the best writing about climbing isn’t really about the climbing at all. The raw facts of an ascent, especially if nothing major went wrong, don’t usually form a good narrative! Other modalities like video and images fare a bit better, since they’re inherently more engaging. But in some of my favourite pieces of writing about climbing, the climb is just a vehicle for the real story arc. I’m thinking of Nick Bullock in Tides, or Mark Twight in Kiss or Kill.

item.title: Ventricle
item.description:
13th February 2022, Number 1 Buttress, Coire an Lochan

Rob and I wandered in to lochan for an easy day, and stumbled out well after dark after ventricle. A post of few words and many pictures:

Great view of most of the route from number 2 buttress. I’m just getting stuck into the crux move of pitch 2, where you rock up onto a big flat foothold and stretch miles over left for a mediocre hook which lets you lower yourself into the corner. Then you get a breather on a ledge before a wild overhanging groove up to the belay. Pitch 1 was too desperate for any pictures! Conditions low down were just the way we like it- marginally acceptable..

item.title: Neanderthal
item.description:
11th February 2022, Lost Valley Buttress

In January I managed to switch my work down to a four day week. I really enjoy my job most of the time but there’s probably nothing which I want to spend 40 hours, 5 days a week doing year-round. I see it getting more and more common in my peer group and I really hope it catches on in this post-single-earner-per-household world. It was pretty handy this winter as it felt like there was often decent conditions for mixed climbing on the Friday before everything would deteriorate a bit on the weekend. On one such Friday me, Will and Rob headed up to Lost Valley Buttress for a look at Neanderthal.

item.title: Sioux Wall
item.description:
Sioux wall. Ben Nevis. I must have walked past the sheer face at least ten times on my way to other climbs in Coire na Ciste before even imagining that a winter route would find a way up there. But this time looking up with the confidence that we could get up it, the line was hard to miss.

North face car park, 6 AM: My alarm drags me awake to the sound of rain. Lying there in the darkness I feel totally unwilling to leave the damp tent. Psyche is low. I hear Will moving around and pretend to be asleep, while staring at the rain radar on my phone, seething. I don’t get up until a precious hour has elapsed, and at least there’s enough daylight to see the rain.

item.title: Un Poco Loco
item.description:
18th January 2020, Church Door Buttress, Bidean Nam Bian

January was a tough month for winter climbing in Scotland. An exceptionally warm Christmas period completely stripped the mountains, and for the rest of the month the weather seemed to alternate between massive storms and deep thaws which stopped any build up. Despite several attempts to get out, this was the only route I managed in the whole month. Luckily it was a belter!

item.title: Auricle and Ventriloquist
item.description:
30th November 2019, Coire an Lochan, Cairngorms, Scotland

Reflecting on the first few months of the scottish winter season in front of the fire in my parent’s house, these two routes spring straight to mind. Ventriloquist was a desperate no-holds-barred fight, with the odds of success ebbing and flowing on every move. I was buzzing for days afterwards, but at the same time that kind of effort empties the tank too much to be sustainable more than a handful of times a season. Auricle on the other hand was a good notch more comfortable, classic type I fun.
